name: San Bernardino County, California
tagline: The largest county in the contiguous U.S. by land area, serving ~2.2M residents — central IT runs through the Innovation & Technology Department (ITD), with technology buys flowing through county Purchasing and California/national cooperative contracts.
overview: San Bernardino County is the largest county in the contiguous United States by land area (~20,000 sq mi) and home to roughly 2.2 million residents, governed by an elected five-member Board of Supervisors and run day-to-day by an appointed Chief Executive Officer. Enterprise technology is centralized in the Innovation & Technology Department (ITD), led by the County CIO, which delivers infrastructure, applications, data, GIS, and the countywide information-security program across dozens of departments. The county is a self-contained buying center: it sets its own technology direction and procures through its Purchasing Department rather than a state portal, while remaining eligible to leverage California statewide (CMAS) and national cooperative vehicles. For a Dell SLG seller, this is a large, sophisticated single-account opportunity — a recognized 'Digital County' that has leaned into AI productivity tooling, analytics, GIS, and security maturity — where the relationship map runs CEO → CIO/ITD → CISO and Purchasing.

budget.intro: San Bernardino County's total countywide budget is roughly $10.9B for FY2026-27 (a near-flat budget versus the prior year), funded largely by state and federal program dollars; the discretionary general funding the county controls directly — primarily property tax — is a much smaller slice. IT spend is embedded within ITD's appropriation and individual department budgets rather than published as a single line, so size deals against department initiatives, not a headline IT number.

stats.label: FY2025-26 budget
stats.note: Adopted with ~$205M in new community investments

stats.label: Budgeted positions
stats.note: FY26-27 payroll; large internal IT user base to support

notes: Much of the budget is restricted state/federal program funding; discretionary general funding (mostly property tax) is the smaller pool the county steers directly.
IT capital and refresh dollars are distributed across ITD and department budgets — tie proposals to named programs (AI, analytics, security, GIS) rather than a single IT appropriation.

initiatives.name: AI productivity & developer tooling
initiatives.summary: ITD deployed GitHub Copilot in its Solutions Development team, reporting roughly a 30% reduction in project timelines, and uses real-time translation (Wordly) for board meetings. An early, pragmatic AI-adoption posture — a hook for AI-ready endpoints, workstations, and infrastructure.

initiatives.name: Data & analytics modernization
initiatives.summary: The county expanded analytics on Microsoft Dynamics 365 and stood up a Business Relationship Management function to align IT with departments. Analytics and data workloads drive demand for compute, storage, and data-platform infrastructure.

initiatives.name: Countywide information security program
initiatives.summary: The security program added a Threat Intelligence team (2024) and runs progressive phishing-simulation testing. Security maturity is rising — an opening for endpoint security, identity, resilient storage, and zero-trust infrastructure conversations.

initiatives.name: GIS & enterprise geospatial / drone program
initiatives.summary: An enterprise Esri agreement gives all departments GIS access, and the San Bernardino International Airport UAS Center standardizes drone governance — geospatial and imagery workloads that need scalable compute and storage at the edge and core.

procurement.intro: The county buys through its own Purchasing Department (with an ePro electronic procurement system) rather than a state agency portal, and is eligible to ride California statewide and national cooperative contracts to avoid one-off RFPs. Confirm Dell's coverage on the vehicles the county actually uses before forecasting.

vehicles.name: County Purchasing / ePro
vehicles.detail: The county's central Purchasing Department issues solicitations and runs the ePro system; vendor registration and competitive thresholds apply for direct buys.

vehicles.name: California statewide contracts (CMAS / Cal-PCA)
vehicles.detail: As a California public entity, the county can leverage California Multiple Award Schedules (CMAS) and statewide commodity/cooperative agreements to streamline technology purchases.

vehicles.name: National cooperatives (NASPO ValuePoint, OMNIA, etc.)
vehicles.detail: Counties commonly buy IT off national cooperative vehicles; confirm which co-ops the county uses and that Dell is positioned on them for the relevant categories.

powerStructure.name: Luther Snoke
powerStructure.title: Chief Executive Officer
powerStructure.note: Appointed permanent CEO after serving interim from 2023; runs county operations and the budget. Top executive sponsor for enterprise-scale technology priorities.

powerStructure.name: Lynn Fyhrlund
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Officer (ITD)
powerStructure.note: CIO since 2023 (formerly Milwaukee County CIO); owns enterprise IT strategy, infrastructure, and modernization. The primary technology relationship.

powerStructure.name: Innovation & Technology Department (ITD)
powerStructure.title: Central IT delivery
powerStructure.note: Delivers infrastructure, applications, data, GIS, and shared services countywide — the main buying center for enterprise infrastructure and devices.

powerStructure.name: Countywide Information Security Program / CISO
powerStructure.title: Security & threat intelligence
powerStructure.note: Owns the security program, threat-intel team, and phishing testing; confirm the current CISO by name. Owner for endpoint, identity, and resilience plays.

powerStructure.name: Purchasing Department
powerStructure.title: County procurement
powerStructure.note: Runs solicitations and the ePro system; the contracting path and the gatekeeper for vehicle eligibility and competitive process.

news.title: Board adopts ~$10.9B FY2026-27 budget (near flat)
news.impact: A flat budget signals disciplined spending — lead with ROI, consolidation, and lifecycle refresh rather than net-new expansion.

news.title: FY2025-26 budget adopted at ~$10.5B with ~$205M in new investments
news.impact: Community-investment dollars create program-tied technology demand — map proposals to funded initiatives.

news.title: County recognized in GovTech Digital Counties 2025 for AI and data
news.impact: Validates an innovation-forward IT posture — credible runway for AI-ready infrastructure and analytics conversations.

news.title: Information security program adds Threat Intelligence team
news.impact: Security maturity is rising — an opening for endpoint security, resilient storage, and zero-trust infrastructure.

dellActions: Build the relationship map CEO (Snoke) → CIO/ITD (Fyhrlund) → CISO and Purchasing; treat this as a large, self-contained single-account motion.
Lead with AI-ready client and infrastructure tied to the county's existing AI/Copilot and analytics momentum — frame around productivity ROI under a flat budget.
Confirm Dell's positioning on the vehicles the county actually buys through (county Purchasing/ePro, CMAS, and national cooperatives) before forecasting.
Engage the information-security program with endpoint security, identity, and resilient/recovery storage as the threat-intel and zero-trust posture matures.
Anchor proposals to funded department initiatives (data/analytics, GIS/drone, public safety) rather than a single IT line, given a near-flat overall budget.
